---
title: "Water"
description: "Fresh water across Canada — its major lakes, rivers and watersheds"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os, json
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import choropleth_map, choropleth_groups_map, choropleth_categorical, add_city_markers, add_boundaries

MAP_CONFIG = {"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True,
              "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]}
```

## A country of fresh water

Canada holds close to **20% of the world's fresh water** and **more lake area than any other country on Earth**. The map below highlights the **40 largest lakes and rivers**, shaded by surface area: the shared **Great Lakes** along the southern border, and the great northern lakes — **Great Bear** and **Great Slave** in the Northwest Territories, and **Athabasca**, **Winnipeg** and **Reindeer** across the Prairies and Canadian Shield. The three largest **rivers** in the set — the Mackenzie, Yukon and Peace — are drawn in a flat blue, so they read as flowing water. Hover any feature for its name and surface area; zoom and pan to explore.

```{python}
#| echo: false
lakes_geo, lakes_csv = "data/geo/lakes_major.geojson", "data/geo/lakes_major.csv"
if os.path.exists(lakes_geo) and os.path.exists(lakes_csv):
    lg = json.load(open(lakes_geo))
    ld = pd.read_csv(lakes_csv, dtype={"lakeid": str})
    # A few of the "largest waterbodies" are rivers (Mackenzie, Yukon, Peace). Split them
    # out and draw them as flat blue ribbons so they read as flowing water rather than
    # lakes or — with the dark lake outline — political borders.
    is_river = ld["name"].str.contains("River")
    lakes_df, rivers_df = ld[~is_river], ld[is_river]
    fig = choropleth_map(lg, lakes_df, "lakeid", "area_km2", name_col="name",
        colorbar_title="Surface area", colorscale="Blues", value_suffix=" km²", value_fmt=",.0f",
        log=True, center={"lat": 59, "lon": -98}, zoom=2.4,
        line_color="#222", line_width=0.8,
        source_note="Source: Natural Resources Canada, Atlas of Canada 1:1,000,000 waterbodies. The 40 largest named natural lakes and rivers touching Canada (shared Great Lakes included; reservoirs excluded). Surface area approximate, from 1:1M-scale outlines; lakes coloured on a log scale.")
    if len(rivers_df):
        river_area = rivers_df["area_km2"].map(lambda v: f"{v:,.0f}").tolist()
        fig.add_trace(go.Choroplethmapbox(
            geojson=lg, locations=rivers_df["lakeid"].tolist(), z=[1] * len(rivers_df),
            zmin=0, zmax=1, featureidkey="id",
            colorscale=[[0, "#6baed6"], [1, "#6baed6"]], showscale=False,
            marker=dict(line=dict(width=0.5, color="#3182bd"), opacity=0.85),
            customdata=list(zip(rivers_df["name"].tolist(), river_area)),
            hovertemplate="<b>%{customdata[0]}</b> (river)<br>Surface area: %{customdata[1]} km²<extra></extra>",
            name="rivers"))
    # Light province/territory boundaries for orientation (a touch lighter than before);
    # their southern edges trace the Canada–US border, while coasts come from the basemap.
    if os.path.exists("data/geo/prov_2021.geojson"):
        add_boundaries(fig, json.load(open("data/geo/prov_2021.geojson")), color="#b4b8bd", width=0.6)
    fig.show(config=MAP_CONFIG)
else:
    print("Lakes data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_geography`.")
```

## Where the water goes

Provinces are political lines; **watersheds** are the natural ones. Almost every drop of
rain or snow in Canada eventually drains toward one of five destinations — the **Pacific**,
the **Arctic**, **Hudson Bay**, the **Atlantic**, or (for a small corner of the southern
Prairies) the **Gulf of Mexico**. Seen this way the country reorganises itself entirely:
the **Hudson Bay** basin alone gathers water from Alberta to Quebec. The map groups
Statistics Canada's 25 drainage regions by the ocean each one ultimately reaches, with the
major **rivers** drawn on top so you can trace the actual flow — the Saskatchewan and Nelson
feeding Hudson Bay, the Mackenzie draining the North to the Arctic, and the Fraser and
Columbia running to the Pacific.

```{python}
#| echo: false
dgeo, dcsv = "data/geo/drainage_2017.geojson", "data/geo/statcan_drainage.csv"
if os.path.exists(dgeo) and os.path.exists(dcsv):
    dg = json.load(open(dgeo))
    dd = pd.read_csv(dcsv, dtype={"dr_code": str})
    oda_colors = {"Pacific Ocean": "#3182bd", "Arctic Ocean": "#80cdc1",
                  "Hudson Bay": "#542788", "Atlantic Ocean": "#2ca25f",
                  "Gulf of Mexico": "#e6550d"}
    fig = choropleth_categorical(dg, dd, "dr_code", "oda_name", name_col="dr_name",
        ordered=list(oda_colors), color_map=oda_colors, legend_title="Drains to",
        center={"lat": 62, "lon": -96}, zoom=2.2,
        source_note="Source: Statistics Canada — Drainage regions of Canada (16-201-X); rivers from the Natural Resources Canada Atlas of Canada (1:15,000,000). Drainage regions grouped by the ocean drainage area each flows to; major rivers drawn on top.")
    rgeo = "data/geo/rivers_major.geojson"
    if os.path.exists(rgeo):
        add_boundaries(fig, json.load(open(rgeo)), color="#0d2b5e", width=0.9, below=None)
    fig.show(config=MAP_CONFIG)
else:
    print("Drainage data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_geography`.")
```

## Download data

- [Major lakes — surface area (CSV)](../data/geo/lakes_major.csv) — NRCan Atlas of Canada 1:1,000,000 waterbodies
- [Drainage regions (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_drainage.csv) — Statistics Canada, 16-201-X
- [Major rivers (GeoJSON)](../data/geo/rivers_major.geojson) — NRCan Atlas of Canada, 1:15,000,000
